reviewer: agent build+matrix pass (runnability verified standalone AND on-page in headless Chrome; first-visit Download → real correct verdict, reload auto-inits with no button; desktop+mobile layout checked at 0px overflow), revision 1

rubric.evidence: RUNNABILITY VERIFIED twice (headless Chrome, WASM). Standalone probe: AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('google-bert/bert-base-german-cased') + AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('oliverguhr/german-sentiment-bert',{dtype:'fp32'}) loaded and returned a real 3-class softmax with correct, well-separated polarity across 5 cases — 'Das Essen war fantastisch…' → positive 1.000; 'Der schlechteste Service…' → negative 0.999; 'Die Lieferung kam pünktlich an.' → neutral 1.000. On the built page: fresh profile showed the Download state + button, clicking it reached state='ready', and the auto-run classified 'Ich liebe diesen Ort, das Essen war unglaublich gut!' → Positiv 99.9% (Negativ 0.1% / Neutral 0.0%) at 85 ms on WASM, with ZERO console errors and ZERO failed network requests.
rubric.notes: Inference runs off the main thread in a dedicated module Worker (new Worker(worker.js)). NB: the weights repo ships no tokenizer.json (only vocab.txt), so transformers.js can't build a tokenizer from it directly — verified failure 'Could not locate file: tokenizer.json'. The model is a fine-tune of google-bert/bert-base-german-cased and the two vocab.txt files are BYTE-IDENTICAL (30000 entries, same order; differ only by a trailing newline), so we load the identical tokenizer from the base repo and the real weights from the fine-tune — a legitimate AutoTokenizer + AutoModel load, confirmed to give correct output.

rubric.evidence: Expected output = logits over the model config id2label (0 positive · 1 negative · 2 neutral); the page softmaxes them to a {positive,negative,neutral} distribution. The probe and the page both returned exactly that with semantically correct polarity. The page renders the full 3-class distribution and a verdict pill.
rubric.notes: A German-native 3-class classifier — semantically distinct from the English DistilBERT SST-2 (2-class), the Spanish RoBERTuito, and the multilingual XLM-R demos.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance declares the exact weights model id (oliverguhr/german-sentiment-bert, linked), the tokenizer repo (google-bert/bert-base-german-cased, linked, with the byte-identical-vocab rationale), the task (text-classification), backend (WASM), quantisation (fp32, onnx/model.onnx — the only export the repo ships), size (~437 MB) and license (MIT). Inference is off the main thread.
rubric.notes: fp32 is honestly declared as ~437 MB (larger than a quantized model) — there is no q8 export for this model on HF.

rubric.evidence: The page loads through the shared createModelLoader auto-init architecture. Verified on-page: first visit (fresh profile) showed state='download-required' with a Download button; after downloading + validating, a reload took the auto-init path — state reached 'ready' with NO Download button rendered (reloadAutoInitNoDownload=true).
rubric.notes: modelId keyed on oliverguhr/german-sentiment-bert so the ~437 MB weights cache is tracked for the never-silently-re-download guarantee; the small tokenizer files load on init. Per-model clear-cache control offered once ready.

rubric.evidence: Loading/ready/scoring state is announced in role=status aria-live regions; the shared loader exposes a Retry recovery path on failure; a Scoring…/Done status line reports progress. Console + network stayed clean across first-visit download and cached reload.
rubric.notes: Errors surface on the page (status .err), not only the console.

rubric.evidence: Every control is real and keyboard-operable: the textarea (as-you-type, debounced 350ms), one-tap German sample chips, and the 'Explain the last verdict' occlusion-attribution button. The practical page adds a batch board + worst-first sort + positive-share meter; wild groups negation/sarcasm/dialect/compound chips; multi-model runs two models side by side.
rubric.notes: Same control pattern as the verified spanish-sentiment / multilingual-sentiment demos.

rubric.evidence: No canned output — the worker runs the real transformers.js AutoModelForSequenceClassification forward pass; verdict, class bars and word-level occlusion attribution are all derived from real forward passes (N+1 variants batched into one model call for attribution). The probe confirmed the exact scores the UI renders.

All buttons have accessible names; sample chips are real buttons with ≥2.4rem min tap height; probability bars use labelled rows; class labels are bilingual (German + English). Live regions use role=status aria-live. Light+dark use the WCAG-AA design system (dark screenshot captured).

Verified 0px horizontal overflow at mobile (360×740, DPR3, touch) and clean desktop (1280×800) in headless Chrome. Layout uses flex-wrap + min-inline-size:0 on the probability grid, sample rows and the multi-model two-column grid (repeat(auto-fit,minmax)); textarea inline-size:100%; chips wrap and cap at max-inline-size:100%.
rubric.notes: Screenshots captured at both classes; support seeded via responsive-check.

rubric.evidence: Inference runs off the main thread in a Web Worker; the as-you-type path is debounced (350ms) so keystrokes never trigger a blocking forward pass; occlusion attribution is capped at 40 words and batched into ONE model call. No main-thread inference loop. On-page single-classify latency measured 85 ms.
rubric.notes: This is an fp32 BERT-base, so a single forward pass is heavier than a q8 model; the debounce + single-batched-attribution keep INP low. Occlusion over long pasted text is bounded at 40 variants.

guidanceConsulted.query: keep INP low as-you-type input without blocking the main thread
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move heavy work off the main thread and/or debounce and yield so input handling doesn't block; keep long loops bounded.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — all inference is in a Web Worker; the as-you-type classify is debounced 350ms; the occlusion attribution is capped (40 words) and batched into one model call.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: worker.js off-thread; debounce() in sentiment.js; console clean; 85 ms single classify

guidanceConsulted.query: responsive control panel and layout without horizontal overflow
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Fluid flex/grid with flex-wrap + min-inline-size:0 and intrinsic sizing; logical properties; avoid fixed widths.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — probability grid, sample rows and the multi-model grid use flex-wrap / auto-fit minmax with min-inline-size:0; textarea inline-size:100%; chips cap at max-inline-size:100%.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: 0px mobile overflow measured at 360×740 in headless Chrome

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible live region status updates for loading progress
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Announce loading/ready/error via role=status aria-live; label every control.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — the shared loader + page status use role=status aria-live; sample chips and buttons carry accessible names; class labels are bilingual.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use cross-document view transitions for catalogue↔page navigation.

openQuestions: fp32 (~437 MB) is a heavy download for a demo; if oliverguhr (or the community) ever ships a quantized ONNX with a real head, add a q8 path to cut the download ~4×. Recorded so a future wave can pick it up — never fake a smaller model in the meantime.
How far do the German specialist (german-sentiment-bert) and the multilingual XLM-R diverge on stacked negation, dialect and long compounds — the multi-model page is the surface to quantify this.

followUpGoals.goal: If a quantized ONNX of a German sentiment model with a real classification head becomes available, add a q8 build to cut the ~437 MB fp32 download (additive, or an in-place dtype upgrade if the same model/id).
Add a dialect/negation-labelled sample set to the wild page to systematically probe where the German specialist beats or trails the multilingual model.

summary: Revision-1 build+matrix pass for german-sentiment (oliverguhr/german-sentiment-bert, text-classification, fp32, ~437 MB — a GERMAN-NATIVE bert-base-german-cased fine-tuned on ~1.8M German samples; DISTINCT from the English DistilBERT SST-2, the Spanish RoBERTuito, and the multilingual XLM-R demos). RUNNABILITY VERIFIED: the weights repo has no tokenizer.json (only vocab.txt) so transformers.js can't build its tokenizer from it — the model is a fine-tune of google-bert/bert-base-german-cased whose vocab.txt is BYTE-IDENTICAL, so the identical tokenizer is loaded from the base repo and the real fp32 weights from the fine-tune (AutoTokenizer + AutoModel), confirmed correct (positive→1.000, negative→0.999, neutral→1.000 across probes; on-page 'Ich liebe diesen Ort…' → Positiv 99.9% at 85 ms). The page loads clean to state='ready' via auto-init (no Download button on revisit) with ZERO console/network errors; 0px mobile overflow. Ladder: basics, practical (German review board), wild (negation/sarcasm/dialect/compounds), multi-model (german-sentiment-bert vs XLM-R). Honest note: fp32 (~437 MB) is the only export the repo ships — declared as such.
